Buyer's question

AI Rollout Mistakes by Company Size The mistakes shift by stage.

Generic "AI rollout best practices" miss that the failure modes change dramatically by company size. A 15-person company fails differently than a 150-person one. Here's the size-specific guide.

Short answer

At <20 people: too much DIY, no documented learnings. At 20-50: too many tools, no named owner. At 50-150: committee paralysis, plans that never ship. At 150-500: enterprise paralysis, IT bottlenecks, vendor-driven instead of business-driven. Diagnose by size; fix specifically.

By Bill Colbert · Founder, Treetop Growth Strategy
Published May 2026 · More from the library

Under 20 people — the startup trap

Common mistakes

  • Founder is the only AI user; team never gets fluent.
  • All learning lives in one person's head; nothing gets documented.
  • Tools get adopted, used briefly, abandoned, repeated.
  • Founder picks tools by reading TechCrunch; not by business need.
  • AI usage is shadow; no policy; eventual surprise when a customer asks.

What works

  • Founder uses AI heavily AND brings 2-3 team members along.
  • One shared prompt library — even if just a Google Doc.
  • A 30-min weekly demo where someone shows what they've learned.
  • Pick one tool; resist the new-shiny urge for 90 days.

20-50 people — the mid-market trap

Common mistakes

  • Buying 6-10 specialized AI tools that all duplicate Claude/ChatGPT.
  • No named AI lead; "everyone is responsible."
  • Workflows planned in meetings but never shipped.
  • Different teams using different tools; no policy.
  • Hiring a "Head of AI" before there's enough AI work to head.

What works

  • One LLM platform + your existing CRM + transcription tool. Resist the rest.
  • Name one AI lead with 8-12 hrs/week protected.
  • Ship one workflow in 30 days. Then the next. Sequential.
  • Publish a one-page AI policy by month 2.
  • External help for the first implementation if internal resources are stretched.

50-150 people — the growth-stage trap

Common mistakes

  • AI committee with 6-8 people; nothing decided.
  • "AI strategy" engagements that produce decks but no shipped work.
  • Trying to roll out across all functions in parallel.
  • Hiring a Head of AI without shipped AI work first.
  • Underestimating change management.

What works

  • Replace the committee with one decisive AI lead (often the COO or Chief of Staff).
  • Roadmap, not strategy.
  • Sequential rollout: one function per quarter.
  • Hire a Head of AI only after you've shipped 5-10 workflows successfully.
  • Treat change management as 50% of the work.

150-500 people — the enterprise-creep trap

Common mistakes

  • IT/security review takes 6 months for what should take 4 weeks.
  • Vendor-driven roadmaps — buying what vendors pitch instead of building what business needs.
  • AI "Center of Excellence" that becomes a bureaucracy.
  • Pilot purgatory — many pilots, few productionized.
  • Federated rollouts with no shared standards.

What works

  • Fast-track IT review for enterprise-tier tools you've vetted at executive level.
  • Business-driven roadmaps. Vendors serve the plan, not the reverse.
  • Small federation team — not an AI CoE. Standards, not gatekeeping.
  • Bias toward production. Kill pilots that don't have a defined ship date.
  • Cross-functional AI guild that shares prompts and patterns. Voluntary.

500+ people — beyond our advice

At 500+, the playbook is genuinely different. Different governance, different tooling, different risk profile, different vendor relationships. We are not the right advisors for this segment; engagements at this scale should go to firms specialized in enterprise rollout.

FAQ

Where do most rollouts get stuck?

50-150 person range. Too big for founder-led, too small for enterprise structure. Committee paralysis is the most common failure mode.

Should I model my rollout on a bigger company's?

No. Failure modes scale with size. Borrowing a 500-person playbook at 50 people produces paralysis.

What if I'm growing fast and span multiple stages?

Plan for the stage you'll be in 12 months from now, not today.

Should we hire a Head of AI?

Almost never at <100 people. Usually right at 250+. The window in between is judgment-call.

Can outside help fix the wrong-stage problem?

Yes — sometimes the most valuable thing a consultant does is steer you away from over- or under-scoped rollouts.
